summary: This brief post explains the origin of the term 'POJO' (Plain Old Java Object), which Fowler coined with Rebecca Parsons and Josh MacKenzie in 2000. The term emerged during preparation for a conference talk advocating for encoding business logic in regular Java objects instead of Entity Beans. They observed that developers seemed reluctant to use simple objects because they lacked a prestigious-sounding name, so they invented one. The acronym subsequently gained widespread adoption in the Java community.
key_points: POJO stands for Plain Old Java Object
The term was coined in September 2000 by Fowler, Rebecca Parsons, and Josh MacKenzie
It emerged from advocacy for using regular objects over Entity Beans for business logic
The authors theorized that simple objects needed a 'fancy name' to gain acceptance
The term successfully caught on in the industry
money_quotes: We wondered why people were so against using regular objects in their systems and concluded that it was because simple objects lacked a fancy name.
So we gave them one, and it's caught on very nicely.
key_insight: Sometimes the adoption of good technical practices is hindered not by their merit but by their lack of impressive-sounding terminology.
